extra.reasoning: The invariant (opted_out implies not sent) holds in every reachable state. Proof by induction on transitions: Initial state opted_out=false, sent=false satisfies it (antecedent false). The only transition that sets sent:=true is t2 (send_attempt), whose guard requires NOT opted_out; immediately after t2, opted_out=false, so the implication's antecedent is false and the property holds. The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which simultaneously sets sent:=false, so immediately after t1, opted_out=true and sent=false — property holds. To violate the property we would need a reachable state with opted_out=true AND sent=true. Since nothing ever resets opted_out to false, once opted_out=true it stays true. While opted_out=true, t2 is disabled (its guard NOT opted_out is false), so sent can never be set true; the events that can fire are t1 (sent:=false), t3 (sent:=false), and t4 (sent:=false), all of which keep sent false (tick also just increments ticks). Therefore opted_out=true forces sent=false forever after. No reachable execution (of any length, including up to 9 events) can reach opted_out=true with sent=true. The property holds.
